retrieved_docs.title: Kwashiorkor(Archived) -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: In kwashiorkor, mortality decreases as the age of onset of the disease increases. Children may not grow or develop abnormally and may remain stunted. There can be serious complications when treatment is not started earlier in the disease course, including shock, coma, and permanent physical and mental disabilities. Kwashiorkor can be life-threatening if left untreated.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of SAM includes a range of conditions that can mimic the features of marasmus (wasting) or kwashiorkor (edematous malnutrition). Wasting, defined as low weight-for-height, can also result from chronic infections such as tuberculosis, HIV/AIDS, and intestinal parasitic diseases. [38] Wasting is seen in malabsorption syndromes as well, including celiac disease and cystic fibrosis, and in childhood malignancies such as leukemia and lymphoma. Additionally, severe psychosocial deprivation or neglect can lead to inadequate nutritional intake and growth failure.

retrieved_docs.title: Kwashiorkor(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of kwashiorkor is fairly unknown, but diets based mainly on maize, cassava, or rice are frequently associated with the disease. It was previously believed to be due to protein deficiency and low levels of antioxidants and aflatoxins. Evidence for these associations exists; however, efforts targeted to replete diets with high-protein and antioxidants have not been successful. Aflatoxin, previously thought to be the etiology of kwashiorkor, is not always associated with the disease in certain populations. Some factors that are consistently associated with the disease include recent weaning, recent infection (particularly measles), and disruptions in childhood (parental death, temporary home environment, poverty). [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Kwashiorkor(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Many pathophysiological steps are involved in the development of protein malnutrition from starvation. In the past, it was argued that hypoalbuminemia was not the cause of edema in kwashiorkor disease. Scientists performing experiments at that time concluded this because the edema went away with dietary treatment, even before the albumin concentration rose when albumin was given. However, re-analysis of this work has revealed a big error in this conclusion, and indeed, profound hypoalbuminemia was proven to be linked to the development of co-existing edema in the hypovolemic child. [11] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Kwashiorkor(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The World Health Organization has a classification system for evaluating malnutrition severity that determines wasting versus kwashiorkor. They use three clinical measures: the mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC), weight-for-height/length Z score, and presence of symmetrical pitting edema. It is generally accepted that MUAC less than 110 mm is highly associated with mortality in infants younger than 6 months old. Criteria for hospital admission are based on reaching defined cutoffs set by the WHO. Nutritional history, past medical history, vaccination history, and family history are also important to elicit from patients suspected of being malnourished. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The HIV/acquired immunodeficiency virus (AIDS) epidemic in Sub-Saharan Africa has significantly increased the caregiving burden on households, often limiting adults' ability to engage in agricultural work due to illness or the need to care for affected family members. [7] Maternal education is another key factor in the development of childhood malnutrition. Results from one study showed that Kenyan mothers with a primary school education had children with a 94% lower risk of chronic malnutrition (growth stunting) compared to mothers with no formal education. [8] Biological contributors to malnutrition include HIV/AIDS and other infectious diseases. [9] Children infected with HIV have worse nutritional outcomes compared to uninfected children. [10] [11] Malaria is associated with stunting in children younger than 2, but rarely causes marasmus or kwashiorkor. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluating a child for SAM involves a combination of anthropometric measurements, clinical assessment, and laboratory investigations. Meeting any of the following criteria establishes a diagnosis of SAM: Weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) <–3 standard deviation (SD) Mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) <115 mm (or MUAC-for-age z-score <–3 SD) Bilateral pitting edema of nutritional origin [2] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Apply standardized intervention strategies to diagnose and treat children with severe acute malnutrition. Identify the clinical signs, anthropometric indicators, and risk factors associated with marasmus and kwashiorkor to enable early diagnosis of severe acute malnutrition. Differentiate between marasmus and kwashiorkor based on clinical presentation, underlying pathophysiology, and nutritional deficits to guide targeted interventions. Implement strategies to enhance interprofessional collaboration in the assessment and management of children with severe acute malnutrition. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying social root cause of SAM in children is poverty. [5] [6] Poverty may stem from factors such as low socioeconomic status and limited parental education, as well as broader systemic issues, including war, natural disasters, and civil unrest. Poverty directly influences a household's ability to secure a reliable food source for children and supply their caloric and nutritional needs. Family displacement and adverse living conditions—such as inadequate shelter and lack of clean water—undermine the ability to care for children, increasing their risk of infections and diarrheal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, children with SAM often have associated medical conditions that must be identified and treated, including tuberculosis, HIV/AIDS, other infections such as measles and malaria, and micronutrient deficiencies. In particular, the presence of both ascites and kwashiorkor increases the clinical suspicion of coexisting tuberculosis and merits diagnostic evaluation with ascitic fluid analysis and imaging. [39] [40] [41] A systematic approach to the differential diagnosis, starting with a thorough history and physical examination, is crucial for distinguishing SAM from other medical conditions and coexisting diseases with overlapping features, thereby ensuring that children receive appropriate and targeted treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Children presenting with severe edema require evaluation for conditions that may resemble kwashiorkor, including nephrotic syndrome, hepatic disease with hypoalbuminemia, congestive heart failure, and severe anemia. Distinguishing these conditions from SAM is essential, as management strategies differ significantly. In most cases of SAM, a thorough history and physical examination, including anthropometric measurements, will help differentiate between malnutrition and other medical conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Anthropometric measurements involve the accurate measurement of vertical length to a precision of 0.5 cm, weight to a precision of 0.1 kg (100 g), and MUAC to a precision of 2 mm or less. [24] In resource-limited or high-edema settings, MUAC-for-age z-score (MUACZ) and WHZ are used together to improve diagnostic sensitivity, particularly for detecting kwashiorkor. Importantly, although commonly used, weight-for-age less than the third percentile is not a reliable indicator for SAM because it does not consider the effects of stunting on a child's linear growth. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for SAM remains guarded, even with appropriate inpatient or outpatient management. Reported recovery rates in outpatient programs range from 65% to 80%, with a median time to recovery of 8 to 9 weeks, provided compliance and no complications occur. However, a significant proportion of children remain stunted in height or underweight, with persistent deficits in height-for-age, weight-for-age, and muscle mass observed up to 5 years after discharge. [42] Relapse rates range from 3% to as high as 37% within 6 to 12 months, depending on the child's environment, follow-up care, and definition of relapse. Children who have recovered from SAM remain at higher risk of recurrent malnutrition and other illnesses compared to their peers, with a 14-fold increased risk of relapse in the year following discharge. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Initial  management involves a stabilization phase, during which hypoglycemia, hypothermia, dehydration, and electrolyte imbalances are carefully corrected, along with prompt treatment of infections. The standard intravenous normal saline solution contains too much sodium and too little potassium for rehydration. ReSoMal (Rehydration Solution for Malnutrition) is a specialized oral rehydration solution formulated for managing dehydration in children with SAM that can be administered by mouth or nasogastric tube. This solution contains less sodium and more potassium compared to standard WHO-oral rehydration solution, aiming to address the unique electrolyte disturbances in SAM, such as hypokalemia and the risk of sodium overload. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Recognition and Management of Marasmus and Kwashiorkor -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Reliance on ketones, not glucose, as fuel for the central nervous system, with reduced brain growth and delays in global function and development [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Kwashiorkor(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis and management of kwashiorkor are with an interprofessional team that includes the primary care provider, nurse practitioner, dietitian, internist, gastroenterologist, rheumatologist, and cardiologist. It is important to know that this is a chronic and complex calorie deprivation disorder that affects almost every organ in the body. Fluid correction requires care as these children often have poor heart function. Electrolytes disturbances have to be corrected and calories slowly increased. The primary condition causing the calorie deprivation must be treated, otherwise, the child will not recover The outcomes for many children are guarded, especially if the CNS has been affected. Even those who survive may have a significant delay in growth and complete recovery may not be possible. [14] [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Nutritional deficiencies, referred to in Arabic as نقص التغذية, represent a parent category that encompasses a wide range of specific deficiency conditions. This overarching concept includes various forms of inadequate intake or absorption of essential nutrients and serves as the broader classification under which individual deficiency disorders are organized. Recognizing nutritional deficiencies as the parent concept helps clinicians systematically assess and address the diverse manifestations of micronutrient and macronutrient insufficiencies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 121
retrieved_docs.content: formulation design, laboratory evaluation, and patient‑centered nutritional management, and supports clinicians in selecting appropriate supplementation to prevent or treat nutritional deficiencies such as vitamin C deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 692
retrieved_docs.content: Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur, which denotes fetal malnutrition that is not specified as light or small for gestational age, is regarded as a broader category that includes اضطراب لدى الرضع وحديثي الولادة, a disorder affecting infants and newborns. This relationship indicates that the specific condition of fetal malnutrition falls within the wider spectrum of neonatal and infant disorders, underscoring its importance for perinatal evaluation and early pediatric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2698
retrieved_docs.content: Deficiency and other anemia have a relationship with فقر الدم الحاد الناتج عن فقدان الدم, indicating that various deficiency‑related anemic conditions are connected to the severe anemia that results from acute blood loss. This link underscores the importance of recognizing how different forms of anemia, including those caused by nutritional deficiencies, may be associated with or progress to the acute, blood‑loss–induced anemia described by فقر الدم الحاد الناتج عن فقدان الدم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 are both classified as vitamins. As members of the vitamin group, they are directly related to نقص التغذية (nutritional deficiency), highlighting that insufficient intake or absorption of these vitamins can contribute to a state of nutritional deficiency. Recognizing vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 within the broader context of vitamins is essential for identifying and preventing nutrient‑related health problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 260
retrieved_docs.content: The knowledge network describes a hierarchical perinatal classification in which a gestational period of 29–30 weeks defines a live‑born infant (خديج) and places the infant within the broader “liveborn” category; gestational length is an inverse‑isa attribute that determines live‑born status. Disorders associated with short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation (short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation) are children of “CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION” and are linked to “تقييد النمو الجنيني” (fetal growth restriction), “ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}” (birth at extreme prematurity, weight unspecified), and “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” (fetal undernutrition without mention of light or small for gestational age). These conditions are also associated with perinatal infections (“عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك”) and with the generic “حالة غير محددة

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 255
retrieved_docs.content: how “Other deficiency anemias” functions as a central classification node that integrates nutritional, genetic, and hemolytic anemia subtypes while intersecting with autoimmune, metabolic, ophthalmologic, and renal disease domains. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships and clinical linkages supports comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and multidisciplinary management of patients presenting with anemia and its associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1489
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia (فقر الدم) is encompassed within the broader category of deficiency and other anemia, indicating that it can arise from nutritional deficiencies as well as other etiologies. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific subtype that falls under the parent condition of anemia, representing a rapid loss of blood leading to reduced hemoglobin levels. Clinically, anemia is closely associated with the symptom of weakness (الشعور بالضعف), a common manifestation that aids in the recognition and management of the disorder. Understanding that anemia, whether due to deficiency, acute hemorrhage, or other causes, frequently presents with weakness is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION encompass the specific subgroup identified as short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This subgroup is both defined by and classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction). تقييد النمو الجنيني, in turn, is linked to a broader, unspecified condition that arises in the perinatal period, described as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Together, these relationships illustrate that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized components of fetal growth restriction, which itself forms part of a larger, not‑yet‑specified perinatal disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 0 -- Community 17
retrieved_docs.content: Gestational age of 29‑30 weeks is linked to the liveborn outcome, positioning liveborn as a specific instance of Duration of gestation and as the parent concept for disorders relating to short gestation and/or low birth weight. These disorders belong to the composite category “Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation,” which is also classified as fetal growth restriction (تقييد النمو الجنيني) and includes related entities such as Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur (fetal malnutrition without mention of small for gestational age) and ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (birth at extreme prematurity, unspecified weight). Liveborn and its short‑gestation/low‑birth‑weight disorders manifest within the Neonatal qualifier value, an inverse subclass of the Fetal and/or neonatal period that serves as a contextual anchor for a wide spectrum of neonatal pathologies. The Neonatal qualifier connects disorders such as

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur, a Dutch term describing fetal malnutrition without reference to being light‑weight or small for gestational age, is classified as a condition encompassing short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. In addition, this specific form of fetal undernutrition is recognized as having a broader relationship to اضطراب لدى الرضع وحديثي الولادة, an Arabic phrase denoting disorders affecting infants and newborns. Consequently, the condition is understood both as a distinct category of intrauterine growth compromise and as part of the wider spectrum of neonatal and infant health disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2514
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a clinical classification used to identify infants who experience premature delivery, reduced birth weight, or impaired intra‑uterine growth. Within this category, the condition described in Dutch as “foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” (fetal malnutrition without reference to being light or small for gestational age) is specifically classified as a form of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. This grouping helps clinicians recognize and manage a spectrum of growth‑related perinatal problems, even when the malnutrition is not explicitly noted as low weight or small size for the gestational age.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 49
retrieved_docs.content: anemia (Arabic: فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات), reflecting the well‑established relationship that folate deficiency can cause this anemia. By delivering iron together with folic acid and a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, fatty acids, and supportive agents, IRON/MULTIVITAMINS aim to correct iron‑deficiency anemia, prevent or treat folate‑deficiency‑related macrocytic anemia, and address overall nutritional needs, thereby providing a comprehensive micronutrient therapy for patients at risk of hematologic and nutritional disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 547
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION” is defined as a parent category that includes the combined condition “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation.” This combined condition is used as both a descriptor and a classification label for itself, emphasizing its role as a distinct clinical grouping. The specific diagnosis “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” (fetal undernutrition without reference to being light or small for gestational age) is classified under the “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” grouping, indicating that it shares the same underlying features of premature delivery, reduced birth weight, and impaired fetal growth. In addition, “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” has a broader relationship to the Arabic‑language concept “اضطراب لدى الأطفال وحديثي الولادة,” which denotes a disorder affecting infants and newborns. Together, these relationships illustrate how fetal growth restriction, prematurity, and low birth weight are interconnected components of fetal malnutrition and form part of a larger spectrum of neonatal disorders, underscoring the importance of recognizing these linked conditions for comprehensive perinatal assessment and care.
